coredata.dc:title: Novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor for organophosphorus pesticides via carbon dots supported zirconium-based metal organic frameworks
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ying
Wang, Kaifei
Wang, Minglu
Shan, Ruiping
Li, Xiangyang
Zhang, Hongyan
coredata.dc:description: Given the potential adverse effects of organophosphorus pesticides (OPPs) on living organisms, it is imperative to explore sensing technologies that demonstrate high sensitivity and selectivity for OPPs detection. In this regard, a novel ratiometric fluorescence probe has been developed, which employs carbon dots integrated within zirconium (Zr)-based metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), specifically PCN-224@CDs, for the determination of OPPs. The probe achieves precise recognition of phosphorus group sites in OPPs through targeted interactions between the active Zr centers and the phosphorus groups, and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) occurring between carbon dots and PCN-224. Subsequent analyses conducted through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and calculations of adsorption energy further corroborated the occurrence of electron transfer between organophosphorus molecules and Zr groups. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity exhibited a linear correlation with the concentrations of malathion, phoxim, chlorpyrifos, and omethoate with recovery rates in celery and leek samples ranging from 75.51% to 115.74%. This ratiometric fluorescence assay offers a reliable methodology and presents a promising avenue for the advancement of trace risk detection technologies, showcasing exceptional analytical reliability and practicality.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric
Fluorescence
Organophosphorus pesticides
Metal organic framework
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Kinetic analysis of PCN-224 and OPPs
Analysis of binding mechanism of PCN-224@CDs and OPPs
Analysis of binding mechanism using FTIR and XPS
Results and analysis of binding kinetics of malathion and PCN-224

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Key Technology Research and Development Program of Shandong

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key R&D Program of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Fluorescence analysis techniques have attracted attention due to their simplified operation, exceptional selectivity, and remarkable accuracy (Gotor, Ashokkumar, Hech, Keil, & Rurack, 2017; Teerapanich et al., 2017; Wei, Huang, Gao, Lu, & Shi, 2021). Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a type of porous hybrid materials that are composed of metal ions/clusters and organic ligands, which has become excellent candidates for fluorescence sensors due to its porosity, chemical tunability and stability (Guo et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2022). The recognition of OPPs, however, can be achieved through the specific coordination of zirconium (Zr) with phosphate groups (J. H. Wang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2022). A prototypical representative with commendable loading capacity is the Zr-based porphyrin MOF PCN-224, a porous nanomaterial composed of Zr6 cluster and 5,10,15,20-tetrakis (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin (TCPP), which improves both water solubility and fluorescence detection sensitivity (Feng, Tang, Wu, & Zhang, 2024). Majority Zr-based MOF fluorescence sensors rely on single response signal of either fluorescence enhancement or quenching, which is susceptible to environmental factors (pH, temperature, etc.), thereby compromising accuracy and reliability (Gotor et al., 2017; Haas et al., 2010; Teerapanich et al., 2017; Wei et al., 2021). In contrast, ratiometric fluorescence sensors are capable of simultaneously measuring two fluorescence signals at distinct wavelengths, and achieve quantitative detection of the target through the correlation between the fluorescence intensity ratio and the target concentration. These sensors offer the advantage of two self-calibrating signals to reduce the influence of external factors and enhance sensitivity. Consequently, the preparation of ratiometric fluorescence probes with stability and sensitivity is the key to the successful construction of fluorescence methods (Liu et al., 2020; Qin et al., 2023). As a common nano fluorescent material, carbon dots (CDs) have the advantages of facile synthesis, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility and photochemical stability, suggesting their potential for biomedical applications. These CDs can be combined with MOF to prepare innovative and sensitive proportional fluorescent probes, sparking curiosity among scientists. However, there is limited information available about the use of CDs-supported MOF sensors for the ratiometric fluorescence detection of OPPs.This study was supported by the Key Program of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U22A20550) and the Key R&D Program of Shandong Province (2023CXGC010712).
This study was supported by the Key Program of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U22A20550) and the Key R&D Program of Shandong Province (2023CXGC010712).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Synthesis process of PCN-224@CDs. (B) TEM electron microscope images of PCN-224@CDs. (C) Zeta potential and (D) FT-IR mapping of the materials. (E) C 1s, (F) O 1s and (G) Zr 3d spectrum of PCN-224@CDs.

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) mapping of materials (A) PCN-224 and (B) PCN-224@CDs with malathion; XPS spectrum of (C) O 1s, (D) Zr 3d and (E) P 2p spectrum in the reaction complex of PCN-224 and malathion; XPS spectrum of (F) O 1s, (G) Zr 3d and (H) P 2p spectrum in the reaction complex of PCN-224@CDs and malathion.

Molecular structure of (A) PCN-224 (green circle in the structure represents the Zr atom) and (B) malathion (yellow circle in the structure represents the S atom). (C) Adsorption energy analysis of PCN-224 and malathion. (D) Differential charge analysis of PCN-224 and malathion.

(A) UV absorption of CDs, PCN-224 and PCN-224@CDs; (B) UV absorption of PCN-224 and porphyrin molecules; (C) Fluorescence absorption of CDs and PCN-224 before and after the addition of malathion; (D) Fluorescence absorption of PCN-224, porphyrin and PCN-224@CDs before and after the addition of malathion.
(A) Fluorescence absorption spectra of different synthesis ratios of CDs and PCN-224; Optimization of (B) reaction time, (C) reaction temperatures, (D) the concentration of HEPES and (E) PCN-224@CDs.

Fluorescence spectrum images of different concentrations of (A) malathion, (B) phoxim, (C) chlorpyrifods and (G) omethoate; Standard curve for (D) malathion, (E) phoxim, (F) chlorpyrifods and (H) omethoate detection. (I) Results of nine different pesticides by fluorescence sensor.
Schematic diagram of the PCN-224@CDs ratiometric fluorescent probe for OPPs detection.
Determination of OPPs in vegetable samples determined by PCN-224@CDs.

head.ce:title: Novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor for organophosphorus pesticides via carbon dots supported zirconium-based metal organic frameworks

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Animal Resistance Biology, Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety of Shandong Normal University, College of Life Sciences, Shandong Normal University, Jinan, 250014, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Animal Resistance Biology
Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety of Shandong Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Animal Resistance Biology, Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety of Shandong Normal University, College of Life Sciences, Shandong Normal University, Jinan 250014, PR China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Jinan, 250033, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Jinan 250033, PR China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Province Institute for the Control of Agrochemicals, Jinan, 250131, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Province Institute for the Control of Agrochemicals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Province Institute for the Control of Agrochemicals, Jinan 250131, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Given the potential adverse effects of organophosphorus pesticides (OPPs) on living organisms, it is imperative to explore sensing technologies that demonstrate high sensitivity and selectivity for OPPs detection. In this regard, a novel ratiometric fluorescence probe has been developed, which employs carbon dots integrated within zirconium (Zr)-based metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), specifically PCN-224@CDs, for the determination of OPPs. The probe achieves precise recognition of phosphorus group sites in OPPs through targeted interactions between the active Zr centers and the phosphorus groups, and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) occurring between carbon dots and PCN-224. Subsequent analyses conducted through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and calculations of adsorption energy further corroborated the occurrence of electron transfer between organophosphorus molecules and Zr groups. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity exhibited a linear correlation with the concentrations of malathion, phoxim, chlorpyrifos, and omethoate with recovery rates in celery and leek samples ranging from 75.51% to 115.74%. This ratiometric fluorescence assay offers a reliable methodology and presents a promising avenue for the advancement of trace risk detection technologies, showcasing exceptional analytical reliability and practicality.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphate is the primary component of insecticides, herbicides, fungicides and nerve gases, which has been developed as an alternative to chlorinated hydrocarbons due to its simplicity of degradation (Kang et al., 2023; Liang, He, Mo, Guan, & Liu, 2024). Organophosphorus pesticides (OPPs) are widely used to assist yields and profits while satisfying the need of growing global demand for food (Jacobs, Kougias, Louie, & Roberts, 2024). Nevertheless, the risks and safety precautions associated with it often go unnoticed by many individuals (Dirilgen, Herbertsson, O'Reilly, Mahon, & Stanley, 2023; González et al., 2023; Kuiper et al., 2023). The widespread utilization of OPPs has led to the presence of their residues in soil, water, fruits and vegetables, thereby posing detrimental to the nervous systems, reproductive systems and immune systems due to the prolonged accumulation in living organisms (Chen et al., 2023; Ospina et al., 2022; Saurat et al., 2023). Partial OPPs have been categorized by the World Health Organization (WHO) as toxic pesticide associated with the potential risks of genetic mutations and carcinogenesis (Kaur et al., 2023; Sun et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2022). Therefore, it is imperative to establish a simple, sensitive and accurate method for OPPs detection to mitigate the risks associated with inadvertent exposure and misuse.

Presently, traditional methodologies for OPPs detection primarily involve instruments like high performance gas chromatography (GC) and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) (Brovini, Martucci, de Oliveira, Oliveira, & de Aquino, 2024; Meng, Liu, Zhao, Zheng, & Wang, 2023; Peng et al., 2024). However, the practical application of these methods is restricted by complex preprocessing procedures, time consumption and specialized personnel requirement. In addition, new bioanalytical techniques for OPPs, such as antibody-based immunoassays and enzyme inhibitory biosensors using acetylcholinesterase or organophosphorus hydrolase have emerged as alternatives (Pang et al., 2015; Sahub, Tuntulani, Nhujak, & Tomapatanaget, 2018). Nevertheless, these biotechnologies have drawbacks including susceptibility to chemical interference, biomolecule deactivation, and the need for substrate sources, etc. (Miao, He, & Zhu, 2010; Noori et al., 2023; Qin et al., 2023) Therefore, it is critical to develop a stable, sensitive, cost-effective and enzyme/antibody-free detection procedure to match the demands of sensitive and accurate OPPs detection.

Fluorescence analysis techniques have attracted attention due to their simplified operation, exceptional selectivity, and remarkable accuracy (Gotor, Ashokkumar, Hech, Keil, & Rurack, 2017; Teerapanich et al., 2017; Wei, Huang, Gao, Lu, & Shi, 2021). Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a type of porous hybrid materials that are composed of metal ions/clusters and organic ligands, which has become excellent candidates for fluorescence sensors due to its porosity, chemical tunability and stability (Guo et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2022). The recognition of OPPs, however, can be achieved through the specific coordination of zirconium (Zr) with phosphate groups (J. H. Wang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2022). A prototypical representative with commendable loading capacity is the Zr-based porphyrin MOF PCN-224, a porous nanomaterial composed of Zr6 cluster and 5,10,15,20-tetrakis (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin (TCPP), which improves both water solubility and fluorescence detection sensitivity (Feng, Tang, Wu, & Zhang, 2024). Majority Zr-based MOF fluorescence sensors rely on single response signal of either fluorescence enhancement or quenching, which is susceptible to environmental factors (pH, temperature, etc.), thereby compromising accuracy and reliability (Gotor et al., 2017; Haas et al., 2010; Teerapanich et al., 2017; Wei et al., 2021). In contrast, ratiometric fluorescence sensors are capable of simultaneously measuring two fluorescence signals at distinct wavelengths, and achieve quantitative detection of the target through the correlation between the fluorescence intensity ratio and the target concentration. These sensors offer the advantage of two self-calibrating signals to reduce the influence of external factors and enhance sensitivity. Consequently, the preparation of ratiometric fluorescence probes with stability and sensitivity is the key to the successful construction of fluorescence methods (Liu et al., 2020; Qin et al., 2023). As a common nano fluorescent material, carbon dots (CDs) have the advantages of facile synthesis, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility and photochemical stability, suggesting their potential for biomedical applications. These CDs can be combined with MOF to prepare innovative and sensitive proportional fluorescent probes, sparking curiosity among scientists. However, there is limited information available about the use of CDs-supported MOF sensors for the ratiometric fluorescence detection of OPPs.

In this work, the PCN-224 and CDs have been synthesized as probes (PCN-224@CDs) with Zr from PCN-224 serving as the active center to develop a ratiometric fluorescence biosensor for the detection of OPPs in vegetable for the first time. The ratiometric detection mechanism is illustrated in Scheme 1 . After the reaction of OPPs with PCN-224@CDs, the Zr in PCN-224 selectively interacts with the phosphorus groups in OPPs, leading to a change in electron cloud configuration that enhances the emission peak at 648 nm in the porphyrin fluorophore of PCN-224. Meanwhile, the 506 nm emission peak of the CDs ultra-small fluorescent nanoclusters is weakened by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Therefore, a selective ratiometric fluorescence procedure for OPPs detection has been prepared by measuring the fluorescence values at 506 nm and 648 nm emission wavelengths to achieve accurate and quantitative detection of OPPs, and provide a more reliable approach for constructing a risk monitoring technique of OPPs residues trace without the need of high-price enzymes or antibodies.

Zirconium chloride octahydrate was purchased from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). 5, 10, 15, 20-tetra-(4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin, H2TCPP was obtained from Zhengzhou Alpha Chemical Co., Ltd (Zhengzhou, China). 4-hydroxyethyl piperazine ethosulfonic acid buffer (HEPES buffer) was purchased from Biosharp Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Hefei, China). Malathion, chlorpyrifos, phoxim, omethoate, avermectin, cyfluthrin, procymidone, 2,4-D and atrazine were bought from Shenyang Research Institute of Chemical Industry (Shenyang, China). N, N-Dimethylformamide, hydrogen peroxide, methanol, hydroquinone, ethylenediamine were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The reagents used in the experiment were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs were collected using HT-7800 (Hitachi, Japan). The size and zeta potential of the material were measured by ZEN3600 (Malvern Panalytical, China) based on dynamic light scattering (DLS) technology. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on an IS50 FI-IR spectrometer (ThermoFisher, USA). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were recorded using UV–vis spectrophotometer (Spectra Max M5, Molecular Devices, China) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan), respectively. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on EACALAB Xi+ (Thermo Scientific, USA). Smaller sizes and higher solubility of materials was processed using VCX500 ultrasonic cell disruptor (Sonics & Materials, USA).

The synthesis method of PCN-224 is based on previous report with minor modifications (Gao et al., 2021). The 600 mg ZrOCl2·8(H2O), 200 mg H2TCPP and 5.6 g benzoic acid were added to 200 mL DMF. After being dissolved by ultrasound, the solution was subsequently stirred at 90 °C under light-excluded conditions for a duration of 5 h.

The 400 mg hydroquinone was dissolved in 7 mL deionized water, and 10 mL 20% H2O2 was added to the mixed solution. Subsequently, 3 mL ethylenediamine was added to the solution for 30 min to form fluorescent nanoclusters. The reaction solution was dialysis with ultra-pure water for 12 h. After dialysis, CDs were obtained by rotating evaporation at 70 °C. Then, the mixture was stored at 4 °C in the dark environment.

The CDs is adsorbed on PCN-224 by electrostatic adsorption, forming the PCN-224@CDs. Firstly, 40 mg PCN-224 was dissolved in 10 mL ultra-pure water. Then, 10 mg CDs was dissolved in 15 mL ultra-pure water, and 1 mol L−1 HCl was dropped into the solution to adjust the pH to 7.0. The CDs solution was heated to 30 °C, and the PCN-224 solution was added while continuously stirring for a duration of 40 min. Subsequently, the solution was centrifuged (12000 rpm, 15 min) and the precipitate was collected after washed by ultra-pure water. Finally, the russet precipitate was collected and dispersed in 1 mL ultra-pure water and stored at 4 °C away from light.

Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR), transmission electron microscope (TEM), particle size analysis and zeta potential, XPS were used to characterize the potential, size, morphology, and dispersion of porphyrins, PCN-224, CDs and PCN-224@CDs before and after reaction with targets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic analysis of PCN-224 and OPPs
ce:section.ce:para:
As a traditional typical OPP, malathion was selected as the target for subsequent binding analysis and condition optimization experiments. The ChemDraw Professional 15.1 tool was used to draw the 2D configuration of malathion molecules, and the Chem3D 15.1 tool is used to convert them into 3D forms. The 3D structure of PCN-224 was constructed using Material studio (MS) software. Then the combined kinetic energy of PCN-224 and malathion was calculated using GGA-PBE functional of Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP) software. Then, the interaction and possible binding sites between them were analyzed. PyMoL 1.7.6 software (http://www.pymol.org/) was used to visually demonstrate the combined results. The adsorption energy (Eads) of PCN-224-malathion was calculated as follows:
Eq (1) Eads = E(ad + sub) − Ead − Esub
Where Eads represents the adsorption energy of PCN-224-malathion, E (ad + sub) represents the energy of the complex after adsorption of PCN-224 with malathion. Ead represents the energy of the energy of PCN-224 and Esub is the energy of malathion.

For the detection of OPPs, 1.5 μL PCN-224@CDs and 1 mL various concentrations of OPPs standard solution (0, 10, 100, 250, 500, 1000 and 1500 ng mL−1) diluted with 15 mmol L−1 HEPES buffer were incubated for 1.5 h. The fluorescence spectra of reaction system were measured in the range of 450–750 nm upon excitation at 418 nm. And the fluorescence intensity values at 506 nm and 648 nm were recorded. The standard curve was drawn with the OPPs concentration as the horizontal coordinate and ΔF as the vertical coordinate to achieve the quantitative detection of OPPs.

The formula for calculating the ratio fluorescence intensity value is as follows:
Eq (2) ΔF = (F0 506/F0 648) – (F506/F648)
Where F0 and F represents the fluorescence intensity of PCN-224@CDs before and after adding the target analyte, respectively.

To evaluate the accuracy and applicability of PCN-224@CDs ratiometric fluorescence probe, celery and leek samples were selected for recovery experiments. Vegetables for spiked analysis were purchased from local supermarket. And prior to analysis, the celery and leek samples were determined by National Standards of China (GB/T 20769) to verify the absence of malathion, chlorpyrifos, phoxim and omethoate. The 2 g homogenized celery or leek samples and 5 mL 15 mM HEPES buffer were mixed, and the mixture was centrifuged (4000 rpm min−1, 2 min), then the supernatant was collected for the next step. The 1 mL of the prepared supernatant was reacted with 1.5 μL PCN-224@CDs dispersion solution at room temperature for 1.5 h for final fluorescence detection.

The spiked recovery rates of malathion, chlorpyrifos, phoxim and omethoate at three different levels (100, 500 and 1000 ng mL−1) were used to verify the practical application of the established procedure.

The relative recovery percentage (RR %) according to the following equation:
Eq (3) RR(%) = (Crs – Cr)/Cs × 100

Crs represents the obtained concentration of the analyte extracted from the spiked vegetable sample, and Cr is the obtained concentration of the analyte extracted in the vegetable sample without spiking and Cs is concentration of the analytes spiked into the vegetable sample.

Synthesis process of PCN-224@CDs has been demonstrated in Fig. 1 A. The TEM images of PCN-224 were composed of irregular crystal structure with diameter of 100 nm (Figs. S1A and S1B). The observed spheroids morphology and size of PCN-224 were in accordance with previous reports (Zhang et al., 2022). The TEM image of PCN-224@CDs was composed of irregular crystal structure with diameter of 150 nm (Fig. 1B and Fig. S1C). The Zeta potential of PCN-224 was measured to be 26.5 mV, while the CDs exhibited a Zeta potential of −16.1 mV. Meanwhile, the Zeta potential of PCN224@CDs was measured to be −3.95 mV (Fig. 1C). Hence, it is speculated that the formation of the PCN224@CDs composite material can be attributed to electrostatic adsorption between PCN-224 and CDs.

The chemical bond and functional group information were measured by FTIR technology. The peaks of PCN-224@CDs at 3440 cm−1 (O-H), 1600 cm−1 (benzene ring in porphyrins) remained unchanged compared with PCN-224. The characteristic stretching vibration of the C=O bond in the free carboxylic groups was enhanced at 1655 cm−1 (Fig. 1D). In the FTIR spectrum, PCN-224@CDs exhibited the characteristic bands similar to PCN-224 at 650 cm−1 (Zr-O), which was also consistent with previous studies. And these results collectively proved that the PCN-224@CDs had been successful prepared (Xu et al., 2023). The Zr-O bond in PCN-224@CDs and PCN-224 remained unaffected, suggesting that the presence of CDs in PCN-224@CDs did not impact the recognition of the Zr binding site in PCN-224.

The element composition of PCN-224 was analyzed by XPS. As shown in Fig. S2C, the Zr 3d5/2 and Zr 3d3/2 of PCN-224 were located in 182.2 eV and 184.5 eV, respectively, which was consistent with other researches (Feng et al., 2024; Zhou et al., 2022). And the spectra of C 1s of PCN-224 showed the characteristic peak of C=N on pyrrole ring at 287.1 eV and a characteristic peak of carboxyl groups on porphyrins at 288.8 eV (Fig. S2A). The spectra of O 1s contained peaks of Zr-O and C=O at binding energy 530.5 eV and 532.6 eV, respectively (Fig. S2B). The aforementioned characterization also proved that PCN-224 had been synthesized successfully. The XPS results of PCN-224@CDs were similar to those of PCN-224 (Fig. 1E and F). As shown in Fig. 1G, the Zr 3d3/2 and Zr 3d5/2 XPS peaks for Zr 3d in PCN-224@CDs were 184.7 eV and 182.4 eV, respectively. Notably, these peaks exhibited a slight shift of 0.2 eV towards higher binding energy compared to those observed in PCN-224. Hence, these characterizations also indicated that PCN-224@CDs had been synthesized successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of binding mechanism of PCN-224@CDs and OPPs
Analysis of binding mechanism using FTIR and XPS
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR technology and XPS were used to analyze the binding mechanism of compound probe with malathion or other organophosphorus pesticides. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the reaction complex of malathion and PCN-224 displayed characteristic peaks at 1091 cm−1 and 963 cm−1, which could be attributed to P=O and P-O-Zr. Meanwhile, the peaks of reaction complex of PCN-224@CDs and malathion displayed characteristic peaks at 1087 cm−1 and 980 cm−1, which could be attributed to P=O and P-O-Zr (Fig. 2B).

The elemental composition and structure of PCN-224@CDs were determined by XPS, and the internal reaction mechanism of the Zr-based MOF prefabricated ratiometric fluorescence probe system was verified. A comparison of the XPS spectra before and after the addition of malathion to PCN-224 revealed P-OH bonds were attributed to 532.6 eV in the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2C), and new 2p peaks at 134.9 eV and 133.9 eV (Fig. 2E), indicating that malathion was successfully incorporated into the framework of PCN-224. Moreover, the Zr 3d spectra of the PCN-224 combined with malathion revealed a shift in Zr 3d5/2 peak from 182.2 eV to 182.5 eV, and a shift in Zr 3d3/2 peak from 184.5 eV to 184.9 eV, respectively (Fig. 2D). Similarly, after malathion was added to PCN-224@CDs, new O 1s peak at 532.9 eV (P-OH) (Fig. 2F) and P 2p peaks appeared at 134.5 eV and 133.4 eV, respectively (Fig. 2H). Zr 3d spectra shown that the peak for Zr 3d5/2 shifted from 182.4 eV to 182.7 eV, and the peak for Zr 3d3/2 shifted from 184.7 eV to 185.0 eV (Fig. 2G). It was speculated that the presence of P-O bond results in the depletion of Zr 3d charge. The aforementioned findings provide evidence for the presence of abundant chelating sites in the mixed system of probe and OPPs. This argument is reinforced by their compatibility with FTIR results furtherly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and analysis of binding kinetics of malathion and PCN-224
ce:section.ce:para: The 3D structure diagrams of PCN-224 and malathion were shown in Fig. 3 A–B. The adsorption energy (Eads) of PCN-224-malathion was calculated according Eq (1). The value of E (ad + sub) was −4723.704 eV, Ead was −215.567 eV, and Esub was −4506.398 eV (Fig. 3C). Therefore, the value of Eads was calculated as −1.739 eV. And the negative adsorption energy indicated that the interaction between malathion and PCN-224 involved stable chemisorption (Consolazio, Hakala, Lowry, & Karamalidis, 2022). Fig. 3D presented the charge density difference of PCN-224 with malathion adsorption from the three-dimensional view. These results shown that the depletion of electron density appears at malathion site and the accumulation of electron density on the Zr atom of adsorbed PCN-224, indicating the charge transfer had occurred from the malathion site to Zr atom (Li et al., 2020). The interaction between the phosphate groups in organophosphorus pesticides and Zr in PCN-224 has been experimentally confirmed, thereby influencing the process of charge transfer.

To obtain the optimal excitation wavelength, ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum was performed on CDs, porphyrin, PCN-224 and PCN-224@CDs, and they all showed significant UV absorption peaks at 418 nm, indicating that their overlapped spectra (Fig. 4 A–B). After the addition of malathion, the UV absorption peak at 418 nm for PCN-224 was significantly enhanced, which could be attributed to the superior coordination ability of the phosphate group in malathion compared to that of the porphyrin ligand with the Zr group. The presence of malathion competed with porphyrin ligands for binding sites on Zr groups could disrupt the charge transfer process and caused the ligands revert back to their electron-free state, resulting in an increase in UV absorption intensity.

CDs exhibited strong fluorescence at 506 nm when they excited with an excitation ultraviolet wavelength of 418 nm (Fig. 4C). The reaction system between CDs and malathion has no obvious fluorescence change compared with CDs, indicating that the presence of phosphoric acid groups does not significantly affect the fluorescence properties of CDs. The porphyrin component of PCN-224 exhibited strong fluorescence at 646 nm, and the intensity of fluorescence unchanged after the addition of malathion (Fig. 4C). The fluorescence intensity of PCN-224 changed significantly at 646 nm upon the addition of malathion, indicating that the porphyrin ligand competitively interacted with the phosphoric acid group of malathion, thereby interfering with the charge transfer process between the ligand and Zr group. Consequently, this led to a return of the ligand molecule to an electron-free state, as evidenced by an increase in fluorescence intensity. Notably, the presence of phosphate groups in malathion did not affect the fluorescence properties of porphyrins (He, Dai, Hu, Lei, & Wang, 2023) (Fig. 4C).

According to Fig. 4A–C, there is a large overlap between the emission spectra of CDs (450–600 nm) and the ultraviolet absorption spectra of PCN-224 (380–460 nm and 500–540 nm), suggesting that FRET processes may occur between CDs and PCN-224. To verify the feasibility of the experiment, the fluorescence signal values of PCN-224@CDs probe were measured before and after reacting with malathion (Fig. 4D). As compared to the single emission probe, the proportional fluorescence probe developed in this study integrated the measurement of two fluorescence types, effectively avoiding external interference and enhancing accuracy and sensitivity in detection (Qin et al., 2023). The results demonstrated a decrease in fluorescence at 506 nm and an increase at 646 nm following the reaction between PCN-224@CDs and malathion, which confirmed the viability of the ratiometric fluorescence determination (Fig. 4D). The change in fluorescence intensity at 506 nm was attributed to the spectral overlap between the absorption profiles of CDs and porphyrin within PCN-224@CDs, leading to the FRET.

To obtain better analytical performance, various detection conditions such as reaction time, reaction temperatures, the concentration of HEPES and PCN-224@CDs were optimized. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 A, the concentration ratio of CDs to PCN-224 was determined to be 0.75:1, and high fluorescence signal values existed at both 506 nm and 648 nm wavelengths under this condition. In addition, the composite ratio of CDs to PCN-224 was changed (the ratio of CDs to PCN-224 gradually increased from 0.75:1 to 12:1), and it was evident that the fluorescence intensity (F648) of PCN-224 regularly decreased with the increase of CDs content (Fig. 5A). According to the energy transfer formula (η = 1 – F/F0, where F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity at PCN-224@CDs at 648 nm in the presence and absence of CDs, respectively), the energy transfer efficiency is inversely proportional to the value of F648. Consequently, it is evident that the energy transfer efficiency enhances with the increasing content of CDs in the PCN-224@CDs. The reaction time between the probe and malathion affected the Δ(F506/F648) value directly. Therefore, the reaction time (0.5 h, 1 h, 1.5 h and 2 h) was optimized and the results showed that the ΔF value decreased after the reaction time exceeded 1.5 h, which was speculated to be due to the degradation of the composite material caused by environmental influence (Fig. 5B). Therefore, 1.5 h was chosen as the optimum reaction time. Meanwhile, fluorescence signal remained unaffected by variations in reaction temperature between the probe and the target (Fig. 5C). It is speculated that the reaction temperature is not the main factor of probe acquisition efficiency. In consideration of operation convenience, 25 °C (room temperature) was selected as the optimal reaction temperature in this study. Additionally, the fluorescence signal was influenced by both the concentration of the buffer and the quantity of added probe. The concentration of different buffer solutions and the quantity of PCN-224@CDs probe will directly impact the variation of ΔF in the system. The reaction equilibrium will be disrupted if the buffer concentration is excessively high. The ion concentration in the buffer system will influence the binding of the phosphate group to the Zr group in the PCN-224@CDs probe, thereby impacting the reaction strength and subsequently affecting the change of ΔF value. As demonstrated in Fig. 5D–E, 15 mmol L−1 HEPES buffering and a probe addition of 1.5 μL were determined as optimal conditions because the highest ΔF was present in these reaction systems.

It has been reported that phosphates such as phosphate-containing drugs can change the fluorescence signal value of the single Zr based material (He et al., 2023). The promotion and inhibition of fluorescence signal responses of composite Zr-MOF materials by OPPs are less studied. In this study, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor was prepared and the specific reaction of phosphate groups with Zr was used as the key recognition element to identify varieties of OPPs, and the fluorescence responses of different OPPs were different slightly. This result was closely related to the structure of pesticide molecules. Four common OPPs were selected to use this assay for detection, and the sensitivity of the sensor to different OPPs was determined.

The fluorescence response at 506 nm of the system was gradually increased and the fluorescence response at 648 nm was gradually decreased with the concentration of OPPs (malathion, etc.) ranging from 0 to 1500 ng mL−1 (Fig. 6 A, B, 6C and 6G). The ΔF506/F648 signal ratio of PCN-224@CDs exhibited a linear correlation with increasing concentration of OPPs. Under the optimum conditions, the ratiometric fluorescence detection of four OPPs were performed with PCN-224@CDs system. As demonstrated in Fig. 6A–D, there was a strong linear relationship between the intensity of ΔF506/F648 and the malathion concentration when the level of malathion ranged from 10 to 1500 ng mL−1 (R2 = 0.955). And the results were similar when the probe was used to detect phoxim (Fig. 6B–E), chlorpyrifos (Fig. 6C–F) and omethoate (Fig. 6G–H). The limits of detection (LOD) for malathion, phoxim, chlorpyrifos and omethoate were 4.57 ng mL−1, 2.69 ng mL−1, 5.88 ng mL−1 and 7.94 ng mL−1 (signal-to-noise ratio of 3), respectively. The analytical performance of the OPPs detection procedure proposed in this study was compared with other reported strategies. As demonstrated in Table S1, LODs of the proposed procedure were better than or comparable to the previous strategies and this assay was highly suitable for the quantitative analysis of OPPs residues.

Selectivity is a crucial criterion for assessing the utility of fluorescence probes. The monitoring of the F506/F648 values is essential for evaluating the selectivity of the fluorescent probes in detecting OPPs. Expect for OPPs, various pesticides such as organochlorine pesticides and macrolide pesticides are widely utilized in vegetable cultivation and pose significant risks to organisms when they enter the food chain (Lourenco et al., 2020; Lv et al., 2024). And the use of these pesticides as interference agents helps to simulate the complex situation in the real environment. Herein, a total of nine pesticides, including four OPPs (1000 ng mL−1) at a concentration of 1000 ng mL−1 and five other types of pesticides (procymidone, cyfluthrin, avermectin, atrazine and 2,4-D as interference chemicals) at concentration of 10 μg mL−1 were chosen for selective spectral analysis. The results demonstrated that with the exception of OPPs, other pesticides exhibited no affinity towards the probe, thereby indicating the remarkable selectivity of the existing ratiometric fluorescence sensor (Fig. 6I).

The PCN-224@CDs sensor was tested in real matrix for its selective assaying of OPPs. And no malathion, chlorpyrifos, phoxim or omethoate were detected in the original vegetable samples. Samples spiked with different concentrations of OPPs were measured according to the proposed procedure, and the results were shown in Table 1 . The concentration of OPPs can be calculated according to the correction curve established in Fig. 6, and the recoveries or RR % for OPPs were determined. Overall, recoveries or RR % of OPPs ranging from 75.51% to 115.74% were achieved and the results were in good agreement with DG-SANTE Guidelines (70% < RR% <120%) (Table 1 and Fig. S3) (Ayazi, Ekhteraei, Pashayi, & Ahmadian, 2022).The obtained recovery values show that there is no significant matrix effect for OPPs measurement in the vegetable samples, which approves the accuracy of the developed ratio fluorescence method for the analysis of OPPs pesticides in spiked samples (Tong et al., 2023; X. Wang et al., 2022). This assay can serve as the universal determination for OPPs residues in vegetables.

In summary, a composite Zr-based MOF fluorescent nanomaterial (PCN-224@CDs) has been synthesized by loading CDs ultra-small fluorescent nanoclusters onto PCN-224. The strong interaction between PCN-224@CDs and phosphorus allows for charge and fluorescence resonance energy transfer, thereby introducing a unique signal amplification strategy. The proposed ratiometric fluorescence sensor has two key advantages compared to previous detection systems: (i) enzymatic/antibody-free sensors are more flexible for use in practical settings, avoiding the need for harsh storage and reaction conditions typically associated with biological recognition molecules; (ii) the fluorescence signal of PCN-224@CDs probe shows a strong linear relationship with OPPs residues even at low levels, satisfying the growing demand for sensitive detection. And this probe can be used to accurately detect OPPs in celery and leek samples, showcasing its potential for tracking OPPs in real samples by optical analysis terminal. In addition, other leaf vegetables with surfaces that are difficult to clean, the edible peels of fruit (such as apples) and tea, etc., will be the focus of performance evaluation for practical applications of the novel fluorescence sensor in the future. In general, the ratiometric fluorescence probe is a valuable tool for detecting OPPs and shows promise for application in screening of OPPs during analytical research. Although the Zr-based MOF sensor is still in early development, it will pave the way for novel applications of MOFs and offers exciting opportunities for the detection of practical food hazards.

Ying Wang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Kaifei Wang: Visualization, Validation, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation. Minglu Wang: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Ruiping Shan: Validation. Xiangyang Li: Validation, Software, Resources. Hongyan Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Key Program of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U22A20550) and the Key R&D Program of Shandong Province (2023CXGC010712).
